explain.rule: Phrasal verbs combine a verb with one or two particles (prepositions or adverbs) to create a new meaning. Types: (1) Separable transitive: you can put the object between verb and particle or after — 'turn the TV off' / 'turn off the TV'. BUT if the object is a pronoun, it MUST go in the middle: 'turn it off' (never 'turn off it'). (2) Inseparable transitive: object always comes after the particle — 'look after the children' (never 'look the children after'). (3) Intransitive: no object — 'break down', 'get up', 'carry on'. (4) Three-part phrasal verbs: always inseparable — 'put up with', 'look forward to', 'come up with'.
explain.why_matters: Phrasal verbs are absolutely essential for natural English. Native speakers use them constantly in conversation. There are thousands, but mastering the most common ones and understanding the grammar rules transforms your English comprehension and production.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew has no direct equivalent of phrasal verbs — Hebrew uses different verb roots for different meanings. This means every English phrasal verb must be treated as a new vocabulary item. The key grammar rule is the pronoun placement: 'turn it off' (correct), 'turn off it' (wrong). Hebrew speakers often say 'look after it' correctly but then say 'turn off it' — the rule is: pronoun ALWAYS goes between verb and particle.
explain.tip: The pronoun rule: if the object is a pronoun (it, them, me, him, her, us), it MUST go between verb and particle. Always. 'Call him back' (correct). 'Call back him' (wrong). 'Pick them up' (correct). 'Pick up them' (wrong). For noun objects: both positions are usually fine for separable phrasal verbs.

good_examples.sentence: Can you turn the TV off? / Can you turn off the TV?
good_examples.audio_text: Can you turn the TV off? / Can you turn off the TV?
good_examples.note: Separable: both positions fine with noun object

good_examples.sentence: Can you turn it off? (NOT: turn off it)
good_examples.audio_text: Can you turn it off? (NOT: turn off it)
good_examples.note: Pronoun must go between verb and particle

good_examples.sentence: She looks after her elderly parents.
good_examples.audio_text: She looks after her elderly parents.

good_examples.sentence: The car broke down on the motorway.
good_examples.audio_text: The car broke down on the motorway.

good_examples.sentence: I can't put up with this noise any longer.
good_examples.audio_text: I can't put up with this noise any longer.

good_examples.sentence: She came up with an excellent solution.
good_examples.audio_text: She came up with an excellent solution.

good_examples.sentence: He turned down the offer. / He turned the offer down.
good_examples.audio_text: He turned down the offer. / He turned the offer down.

good_examples.sentence: I'll look into the matter and get back to you.
good_examples.audio_text: I'll look into the matter and get back to you.
good_examples.note: look into = investigate. get back to = contact again

good_examples.sentence: They had to call off the match due to the weather.
good_examples.audio_text: They had to call off the match due to the weather.

good_examples.sentence: She's finding it hard to get over the disappointment.
good_examples.audio_text: She's finding it hard to get over the disappointment.

bad_examples.reason: Pronouns must go between verb and particle. Never after.
Inseparable phrasal verbs: object always after particle.

bad_examples.wrong: He put up the noise with.
bad_examples.correct: He put up with the noise.
bad_examples.reason: Three-part phrasal verbs are inseparable: put up with + object.

bad_examples.wrong: I can't get my cold over.
bad_examples.correct: I can't get over my cold.
bad_examples.reason: get over is inseparable: get over + object.

bad_examples.wrong: They called the match off it.
bad_examples.correct: They called the match off. / They called it off.
bad_examples.reason: Object between verb and particle, or after particle (nouns only). Pronoun must be in the middle.

exercises.question: Could you turn ___ the lights when you leave? (use the particle only)
exercises.explanation: turn off the lights OR turn the lights off.

exercises.question: She's been looking ___ her neighbour's cat while they're away.
The meeting was called ___ due to the director's illness.
I can't put ___ with this noise any longer.
She came ___ a brilliant idea during the meeting.
The car broke ___ on the motorway.
He turned ___ every job offer he received.
Please fill ___ this form and return it.
She needs to get ___ the fact that he's not coming back.
They've decided to put ___ the meeting until next week.
Can you pick ___ from the station?
exercises.hint: Pronoun must go between verb and particle
exercises.explanation: me up — pronoun between verb and particle.
exercises.distractors: up me / up me at / me at up

exercises.question: She always ___ her promises.
exercises.explanation: lives up to — three-part phrasal verb.
exercises.distractors: carries up / goes through with / follows to

exercises.question: Which is correct?
exercises.hint: Pronoun must go between verb and particle
exercises.explanation: Turn it off — pronoun in the middle.
exercises.distractors: Turn off it. / Off turn it. / It turn off.

exercises.question: He's finding it hard to ___ his new boss.
exercises.hint: get on with = have a good relationship with
exercises.explanation: get on with — three-part phrasal verb.
exercises.distractors: get along / be fine with / carry along with

exercises.question: The charity event ___ over £50,000.
exercises.hint: raise = collect money (not a phrasal verb here — but know the difference)
exercises.explanation: raised — this is a simple verb, not phrasal.
distractors: brought up / came up with / put in

exercises.question: She ___ her application at the last minute.
distractors: handed up / handed across / gave in

exercises.question: I need to ___ the situation more carefully before deciding.
distractors: look at up / investigate into / look it into

exercises.question: They had to ___ the launch because of technical problems.
distractors: put down / set off / call on

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Can you turn off it?'
exercises.hint: Pronoun must go between verb and particle
exercises.explanation: turn it off — pronoun in middle.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'She looks her elderly parents after.'
exercises.answer: She looks after her elderly parents.
exercises.hint: Inseparable phrasal verb: object after particle

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'He put up the noise with.'
exercises.answer: He put up with the noise.
exercises.explanation: put up with + object (object always at end).

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'I called off it at the last minute.'
exercises.answer: I called it off at the last minute.
exercises.explanation: called it off — pronoun in middle.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Could she look after my dog while I'm away?
exercises.hint: Inseparable: look after + object after particle
exercises.explanation: look after my dog — object after particle.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: She came up with a brilliant idea.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: They had to call off the match.
exercises.explanation: call off the match OR call the match off.

speak.sentence: Could you look into the problem and get back to me by the end of the day?
chunks: Could you look into the problem
and get back to me by the end of the day?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Look into' = investigate. 'Get back to' = contact again.

speak.sentence: She came up with such a creative solution that everyone was impressed.
chunks: She came up with such a creative solution

speak.sentence: I can't put up with this situation any longer — something needs to change.
chunks: I can't put up with this situation any longer —

speak.sentence: The car broke down just as we were about to set off on our journey.
chunks: The car broke down just as we were about to set off
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Broke down' = stopped working. 'Set off' = begin a journey.

speak.sentence: Please fill in the form and hand it in at the front desk.
chunks: and hand it in at the front desk.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Fill in' = complete. 'Hand it in' — pronoun between verb and particle.

speak.sentence: She turned down the offer even though it was very generous.
chunks: even though it was very generous.

speak.sentence: He's been looking after his mother since she came out of hospital.
chunks: He's been looking after his mother
since she came out of hospital.

speak.sentence: They had to call off the event at short notice because of the bad weather.
chunks: They had to call off the event at short notice
